The empty tomb does not announce itself quietly. The women come expecting death—they bring spices for a corpse—and encounter instead a proclamation that shatters the logic they had built: "Why do you look for the living among the dead?" This is not gentle reassurance. It is confrontation. Jesus did not drift back to consciousness in the tomb. He rose. And with that rising, everything the apostles thought they understood about the Messiah fractures. He had told them plainly in Galilee—that He must be crucified and rise on the third day—yet when the women report it, the apostles treat their words as nonsense. Even Peter, who runs to verify the claim, leaves wondering rather than believing. We are witnessing not a smooth transition to faith, but the collision of Easter reality with broken, earthbound expectation.

The Emmaus road reveals how deeply that collision runs. Two disciples walk away from Jerusalem, away from the risen Christ and the testimony of their own community, because their hopes have died. They thought Jesus was the one to redeem Israel, and now He is gone. They cannot yet see that His death and resurrection *are* the redemption, though the prophets wrote of exactly this. When the risen Jesus stands beside them and walks with them, they do not recognize Him. Their minds are held back from it. Then He opens Scripture to them—Moses, the Prophets, all the words about the Christ who must suffer and enter His glory—and something kindles in them. But they do not truly see until He breaks bread. In that familiar gesture, their eyes open. He vanishes. And immediately they understand: they had been in the presence of the risen Lord, and Scripture had come alive. What the apostles need is not mere information but encounter—the presence of Jesus Himself interpreting the Word, showing them that suffering and resurrection were never a contradiction of the Messiah's identity but its fulfillment.

When Jesus appears to the gathered apostles, the joy is mixed with terror. They think He is a ghost. Yet Jesus insists on His physical reality with a deliberate physicality—show them His hands and feet, touch Him, watch Him eat fish. This risen body is not ethereal or merely spiritual. It is real flesh and bones. The Resurrection is not the escape of a disembodied soul but the triumph of embodied life over death. And having established that He is truly present, truly alive, truly risen, Jesus opens their minds to Scripture one final time. The pattern becomes clear: suffering, resurrection, forgiveness of sins proclaimed to all nations. The apostles are witnesses to all of it. They are not called to explain what happened but to proclaim it. They are to wait for the power promised by the Father—the Holy Spirit who will enable them to carry this testimony to the ends of the earth. Jesus ascends, but He does not abandon them. He leaves them with His Word opened to them, His mission entrusted to them, and His Spirit about to be sent upon them. They return to Jerusalem filled not with confusion but with joy, praising God because the One who was dead is alive, and the promise now stands before them.

What stops us from joy like theirs is the same thing that stopped them: we do not truly see the risen Christ in Scripture. We read the Gospels as history, the Epistles as doctrine, forgetting that every word points to Him. Jesus opens minds to understand—that is His work, not ours alone. When we encounter the living Christ in His Word, when we see how all Scripture speaks of His suffering and triumph, when we are convinced that this risen Lord stands at the right hand of the Father and lives forever, then we cannot help but worship and praise, even in a world that has not yet bent its knee to His reign.
